narratorScript:
Miles Robinson’s story begins in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and grows through Arlington, where family, community, and sport shaped the foundation of who he would become. His father, an African American musician from St. Louis, brought jazz, art, and the presence of history into the home. His mother, from Connecticut with English family roots, brought a deep care for the environment and a belief in stewardship. Together, they raised Miles in a world where culture, creativity, discipline, and compassion were never separate things.

Soccer entered his life before he was old enough to play. He remembers standing on the sideline at his older sister’s games, watching, waiting, wanting to join. By the time he was old enough, the game had already found him. He loved being outside. He loved running. He loved the feeling of movement, competition, and connection. Very quickly, he realized soccer was more than a sport. It introduced him to different communities, different cultures, and different ways of seeing the world.

Growing up in a diverse environment taught him something that still guides him today: people are more alike than they are different. That belief became part of his identity, not only as a person, but as a player. He learned that unity is not an idea spoken from a distance. It is something built through shared effort, through trust, through showing up for one another.

For Miles, the game has offered both defining triumphs and humbling tests. One of the hardest moments came when he tore his Achilles, just as he believed he might have a chance to play in the World Cup. The injury forced him into a private battle. He had to start again, rebuild again, and compete in silence. It became, as he describes it, “me versus me.” In that darkness, he rediscovered how much he loved the game, how much he loved to compete, and how much pride he could take in doing the work when no one was watching.

Then came the unforgettable moment in 2021, when he scored the winning goal in the Gold Cup Final against Mexico. It was a life-changing moment, not only because of the goal, but because it marked a new understanding of his place in the game.

As a player, Miles describes himself as someone who leads by example. A defender who is hard to get by. A competitor who loves big opponents, big games, and big moments. Before matches, he is calm, focused, and eager. He centers himself through routine, meditation, and visualization.

Yet what defines Miles most is not only what he does on the field. It is how he sees the world. He believes soccer can be a beacon of love, inclusion, and understanding. A game that connects people across countries, cultures, and languages. A game that can bridge gaps.

When he thinks about legacy, Miles hopes to be remembered as a world-class soccer player, but also as something more enduring: a loving person, a positive presence, and a humanitarian.
photographerScript:
When I sit down with a player like Miles Robinson, I'm always hoping to find the person beyond the jersey. What struck me most about Miles wasn't his accomplishments, though they are extraordinary. It was his quiet sense of purpose.

As we spoke, I found myself drawn to the influences that shaped him. A father rooted in music and history. A mother who taught stewardship and care for the world around him. Those threads seem to run through everything Miles does. He speaks thoughtfully, listens carefully, and carries himself with a humility that feels increasingly rare in elite sport.

What stayed with me was his description of recovering from his Achilles injury. There was no self-pity. No dramatics. Just honesty. He described it as "me versus me," a battle fought largely out of public view. In that moment, I wasn't speaking to a professional athlete. I was speaking to someone who understood resilience at a very human level.

Miles also reminded me why soccer matters. Not because of trophies or headlines, but because of its ability to connect people. He spoke about unity, inclusion, and the power of a game that transcends language and borders. It wasn't rehearsed. It was lived experience.

Photographing athletes often begins with the visual. The strength. The intensity. The presence. But the interviews reveal something deeper. With Miles, I saw a competitor who thrives in the biggest moments, yet remains grounded in gratitude and service.

When I look at his portrait now, I don't just see a defender, a champion, or a member of the U.S. National Team. I see someone who understands that legacy is measured by more than victories. It's measured by the lives you touch, the example you set, and the way you carry yourself when nobody is watching.

That's the Miles Robinson I hope people see.
Bio: Miles Robinson credits his upbringing in a beautifully diverse family and community for anchoring his perspective on humanity — it was here he learned that we're all more alike than different. Soccer became a natural bridge across cultures, shaping a lifelong belief in collective unity and purpose. That grounded worldview would later sustain him through the highest stakes of the professional game, navigating the grueling journey from catastrophic injury back to absolute triumph. He learned how to truly compete in the darkness. His ultimate gaze remains fixed on a legacy that transcends sport — to be remembered as a loving person and dedicated humanitarian far beyond the game.

narratorScript:
Julian Hall's story begins like so many others, with a child chasing a ball simply because it felt right. Growing up in New York City, soccer was never confined to a field. It existed on playgrounds, patches of grass, pickup games, and any open space where imagination could transform pavement into a stadium. Long before professional contracts and national recognition, Julian was just another kid finding joy in the game.

What becomes clear when listening to Julian is that his relationship with soccer was never driven by pressure. It was fueled by curiosity and love. He remembers watching his older brother play and thinking, "Why not?" One touch of the ball became another, and before long the game became part of who he was. Barefoot matches on grass fields and crowded seven-versus-seven games taught him lessons that no formal training session ever could. They taught creativity, resilience, and the freedom that comes from simply playing.

Today, that same spirit remains.

Before kickoff, Julian describes feeling excitement more than nerves. There may be butterflies in important moments, but they disappear the instant the ball reaches his feet. For him, the game is still a blessing, an opportunity to do something he genuinely loves. There is a refreshing simplicity in that perspective, especially for a young athlete navigating increasingly high expectations.

What stands out most is how deeply he values the communal nature of soccer. Julian sees the sport as something larger than competition. He views it as a universal language, one capable of bringing together people from every background, culture, and circumstance. In his eyes, soccer succeeds because participation is not reserved for the elite. The game belongs equally to beginners and professionals, children and adults, dreamers and veterans.

That belief was shaped by his experiences growing up in New York City, where pickup soccer often becomes a gathering place for communities that might otherwise never intersect. Different ages, different languages, different stories all converge around a single ball.

As Julian reflects on the role soccer can play in the world, he returns repeatedly to one idea: unity. Not as an abstract concept, but as something visible every time people choose to play together. Every pass, every shared celebration, and every moment of teamwork reinforces a simple truth. The game creates connections.

Julian Hall represents a generation of players who understand that soccer is about more than goals and trophies. It is about joy, belonging, and possibility. His journey is still in its earliest chapters, but the values that guide him are already clear: gratitude for the game, love for the community it creates, and a belief that soccer is at its best when it brings people together.
photographerScript:
One of the things I love most about photographing young players is discovering the person before the accolades arrive. With Julian Hall, what stood out immediately was his joy.

As I listened to him describe falling in love with soccer, I wasn’t hearing a story about academies, rankings, or expectations. I was hearing a story about a kid watching his older brother play and deciding he wanted to join in. There was something beautifully simple about it. Barefoot games on the grass, crowded pickup matches, and endless hours with a ball at his feet. It reminded me that every great player starts in exactly the same place: with wonder.

What struck me most was Julian’s perspective on the game. At an age when many young athletes are consumed by pressure, he still speaks about soccer with gratitude. Right before kickoff, he isn’t overwhelmed by nerves. He’s excited. Excited to play. Excited to compete. Excited to do something he loves.

I was equally impressed by how he talks about soccer’s ability to bring people together. Growing up in New York City, he’s experienced firsthand how the game transcends language, culture, and background. To Julian, soccer isn’t exclusive. It’s welcoming. It’s a place where everyone belongs.

As a photographer, I often search for the thread that defines a player. Sometimes it’s resilience. Sometimes it’s ambition. With Julian, it was optimism. He carries a genuine belief that soccer can unite people and create community.

When I look at his portrait, I see more than a promising young talent. I see a player who still remembers why he started. In a sport that can move very fast, that may be one of his greatest strengths.
Bio: Julian Hall reflects on a life deeply shaped by Harlem, family, faith, and a game that he says took over his life from early childhood. Born to a Polish mother and an African American father, Hall channels a unique blend of cultural heritage on the pitch, playing with a fierce intensity while carrying himself with a rare, quiet humility off it. After etching his name into the history books as the youngest player to score a professional hat trick, the gravity of the achievement caught up with him. To navigate the sudden noise of the spotlight, he turns inward — before every match, he prays simply to stay grounded.

narratorScript:
Max Arfsten's story begins in Fresno, California, a city shaped by hard work, family, and deep Hispanic roots. Growing up surrounded by Mexican culture through his mother's family, soccer was never simply a sport. It was part of the community's identity. On fields across Fresno, the game connected generations, neighborhoods, and dreams.

Long before professional contracts and national team call-ups, Arfsten learned the game in a way that cannot be taught from a textbook. He was often the youngest player on the field, competing alongside older athletes in local leagues and informal tournaments. The matches were intense, creative, and unpredictable. There was little structure and plenty of freedom. Those early experiences shaped the player he would become, one who values instinct, courage, and creativity.

Behind every step forward stood a support system that believed in him before anyone else did. His parents drove countless miles across California, supporting a sport they knew little about but fully embraced because their son loved it. A local mentor, former professional player Milton Blanco, recognized Arfsten's potential and invested his time and energy into helping him grow. Today, that relationship remains as strong as family.

Success, however, has never come without adversity. One of the defining moments of Arfsten's career captures both. In a match for the United States Men's National Team, he experienced the disappointment of conceding a penalty, only to later score his first international goal. The game became a lesson in resilience, proving that setbacks and triumphs often arrive side by side.

As a player, Arfsten embraces risk. He wants the ball at his feet. He wants to challenge defenders one-on-one. Inspired by the flair and freedom of Brazilian football and players like Neymar, he strives to be exciting, unpredictable, and fearless.

Yet his ambitions stretch beyond the game itself. Having become one of the few players from Fresno to reach Major League Soccer and represent his country, he feels a responsibility to inspire those coming behind him. He wants young athletes in underserved communities to see what is possible.

Grounded by family, faith, and a commitment to staying present, Arfsten carries his hometown with him every time he steps onto the field. His journey is a reminder that talent opens doors, but perseverance, humility, and purpose are what carry a player through them.
photographerScript:
There are some players who walk into a studio carrying the weight of their accomplishments. Max Arfsten walked in carrying curiosity, humility, and an easy smile.

What struck me immediately was how comfortable he was in his own skin. There was no performance. No carefully rehearsed version of who he thought he should be. Just Max. As a photographer, those are the moments you hope for because authenticity always photographs better than confidence alone.

As we worked through the session, I began to see the same qualities that define him as a player. There's a quiet fearlessness about him. He talks openly about taking risks on the field, about wanting to beat defenders one-on-one and play with flair. Yet off the field, that confidence is balanced by a remarkable sense of gratitude.

Listening to him speak about Fresno, California, you quickly understand that his story is bigger than soccer. He carries his hometown with him. He carries the influence of his Mexican heritage, the sacrifices of his parents, and the belief of mentors who saw potential in a young player before the rest of the world did.

What I found most compelling was that his ambitions are not centered on fame or accolades. Certainly, he wants to compete at the highest level possible. But beneath that is a deeper motivation. He wants kids from communities like his own to believe they belong in places they may never have imagined.

The camera often reveals things that words cannot. Through the lens, I saw a player who embraces creativity, but also a young man grounded by family, faith, and perspective. Max understands that success is fleeting. He spoke about staying level through both triumph and disappointment, a lesson many veterans spend a lifetime learning.

When I look at the portraits we created together, I see more than a talented footballer. I see someone still writing his story, carrying the pride of Fresno with him, and determined to make sure the next generation knows that dreams can emerge from anywhere.

That authenticity is what made the photographs special. It is also what makes Max Arfsten memorable.
Bio: Max Arfsten carries Fresno with him wherever he plays. Raised in the crucible of a deeply rooted Mexican American soccer culture, he remembers cutting his teeth in the "street style" chaos of local Sunday leagues- battling alongside older, uncompromising players who taught him above all else to “play free.” That education forged a self-described “risk-taker” on the pitch. Unafraid to isolate defenders 1v1, Arfsten wants his legacy defined by expression, hoping to be remembered simply as “exciting” and “fun to watch.” Yet, beneath the technical flair lies a profound sense of purpose. “I want to inspire my community back home in Fresno,” he says, hoping young players see that someone from their own neighborhoods can reach the absolute highest level. For Arfsten, soccer is a rare trifecta: joy, identity, and responsibility, all carried together.
